title: Te Kaitiakitanga o Te Awa: Guardianship of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore their role as kaitiaki through the lens of Ngāti Kahungunu's relationship with Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku, the local stream that has sustained our community for generations. The lesson connects to the pūrākau (legend) of Hinepūkohurangi, the mist maiden whose tears formed the stream, and addresses current environmental challenges facing this taonga.
learningObjectives: Create an original artwork that visually communicates a specific kaitiaki responsibility for Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku using traditional and contemporary materials
Analyze and explain how at least two elements of Māori visual art (such as koru, takarangi, or tohu) are used to represent kaitiakitanga concepts in their work
Collaboratively develop and present a proposal for a community art installation that addresses an environmental issue affecting the Mangakōtukutuku stream
Reflect on how their artistic practice connects to Ngāti Kahungunu values of manaaki, whanaungatanga, and rangatiratanga

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga: Connecting to Our Awa
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku with local kaumātua to hear the pūrākau of Hinepūkohurangi and learn about the stream's significance. They create observational sketches and take photographs of the waterway, noting both its beauty and areas of environmental concern.

activities.title: Toi Māori Workshop: Traditional Patterns of Protection
activities.description: Local artist-in-residence teaches students traditional Ngāti Kahungunu visual motifs and their meanings related to water protection. Students practice these patterns using traditional materials (kōkōwai, charcoal) and contemporary equivalents, exploring how traditional knowledge informs modern kaitiakitanga.

activities.title: Wai Crisis, Wai Solutions: Environmental Art Investigation
activities.description: Using water quality testing data from the regional council, students identify specific issues affecting the stream (e.g., sediment runoff, pollution). In small groups, they brainstorm how art could raise awareness or propose solutions to these specific environmental challenges.

activities.title: Creating Our Kaitiaki Statement
activities.description: Students create individual artworks that express their personal kaitiaki responsibility toward Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku. They must incorporate at least one traditional Māori visual element and one contemporary material or technique, accompanied by an artist statement explaining their choices.

activities.title: Whakatau: Presentation to Community
activities.description: Students present their artworks and collaborative installation proposals to whānau, community members, and local iwi representatives in a gallery-style exhibition at the college marae, explaining how their art contributes to the protection and celebration of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku.

resources: Kaumātua Hemi Potaka (local Ngāti Kahungunu knowledge holder)
Mangakōtukutuku Stream Water Quality Monitoring Data (Hawke's Bay Regional Council)
'Ngā Toi o Te Whenua' by Sandy Adsett (Māori arts resource)
Local clay and natural pigments from the Mangakōtukutuku riverbank

tasks: Completed individual artwork expressing kaitiakitanga of Te Awa Mangakōtukutuku
Written artist statement (300-400 words) explaining cultural and environmental connections
Group proposal presentation for community art installation
Self-reflection on personal growth as kaitiaki through the artistic process

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: Level 5 - Investigate the purpose of objects and images from past and present cultures and identify the contexts in which they were or are made, viewed, and valued
Developing Practical Knowledge in the Arts: Level 5 - Apply knowledge of elements and principles for a specific purpose, using conventions and techniques
Developing Ideas in the Arts: Level 5 - Develop and refine ideas in selected art works in response to a variety of motivations, using appropriate materials, techniques and technologies
Communicating and Interpreting in the Arts: Level 5 - Describe and evaluate how ideas and art works are used to communicate meaning and purpose in particular contexts and for particular audiences
